title: The Resolved Brief
subtitle: The 20-Minute System That Organizes Your Entire Life For The People You Love.
approach: hybrid — assessment reveals starting point, data fields close gaps in real time, triple scoring shows transformation to 100%

welcome.headline: You made the decision most people put off for years.
welcome.subhead: Now let's turn it into a plan your family can actually use.
welcome.body:
Here's how this works: I'm going to walk you through 7 short sections about your life — your finances, your family, your wishes. Most answers are a single tap. When something needs a detail — like a name or a phone number — I'll ask for it right there so it goes straight into your brief.

By the end, you'll have a professional, personalized document your family can actually use if something happens tomorrow. No account numbers. No passwords. Those go on a separate handwritten page you'll seal in an envelope.

This takes most people about 20 minutes. But there's no timer and no pressure — your progress saves automatically. Close the tab, come back tomorrow, pick up right where you left off.

what_to_expect.text: Most questions are a quick tap — no typing needed
When we need a name or number, a field appears — fill in what you know, skip what you don't
Your progress saves automatically — come back anytime
We never ask for account numbers or passwords — that stays with you

welcome.reassurance: Don't worry about getting everything perfect. If you're not sure about something, tap your best guess or skip it. Your brief captures what you know today — you can always update it later.
welcome.footer_note: Your answers are private and encrypted. Only you receive the final document.

sections.title: Foundation & Legal
sections.subtitle: If something happened tomorrow, would your wishes be followed — or would the state decide for you?
sections.intro_message: Let's start with the foundation. These questions determine whether your family would follow your wishes — or be left guessing.

questions.prompt: If something happened tomorrow, would your wishes be followed — or decided by the state?
options: My wishes are clearly documented and legally binding
I have some things written down, but it's incomplete
Nothing is documented — the state would decide
questions.solution_tip: Your brief will document exactly where your legal directives are and who to contact. That's half the battle.
questions.gap_tip: That's exactly why you're here. By the end of this walkthrough, your family will have a clear starting point — even before legal docs are finalized.

questions.prompt: Do you have a will or trust in place?
options: Yes — current and reviewed recently
Yes — but it may be outdated
No

data_fields.prompt: Who is your executor or trustee?

data_fields.placeholder: e.g., home safe, attorney's office, filing cabinet...

questions.solution_tip: Your executor and document location are now in your brief. Your family knows exactly where to go and who's in charge.
questions.gap_tip: A basic will can be created online in about 20 minutes. We recommend Trust & Will (trustandwill.com) — plans start at $159 and walk you through everything step by step. We'll include the link in your brief.

questions.prompt: Does your plan reflect your life today — your current family, assets, and relationships?
options: Fully up to date
Some things have changed since I last updated it
Outdated or never created
questions.solution_tip: Good — your brief will reflect your current situation so nothing is out of date.
questions.gap_tip: Life changes fast. Your brief captures where things stand right now — and your follow-up section will flag what needs updating.

questions.prompt: If someone needed your will tonight, would they know where to find it?
options: Yes — it's in a known, accessible location
It exists, but they'd have to search for it
No one would know where to look
questions.solution_tip: Documented. Your family now knows the exact location — no searching, no guessing.
questions.gap_tip: Once you have one, your brief will document exactly where it lives. One less thing your family has to figure out.

questions.prompt: Have you formally assigned someone to handle your affairs — power of attorney, executor, trustee?
options: Yes — legally documented
Informally — I've mentioned it but nothing is filed
No one has been assigned

data_fields.placeholder: e.g., Wife — POA & executor, Brother — backup executor...

questions.solution_tip: Your brief now lists who has authority and what they're responsible for. Clear chain of command.
questions.gap_tip: This is one of the most important things you can do. Your brief will flag this as a priority follow-up — most states have free POA forms online (10 minutes).

questions.prompt: Would that person actually know what to do first?
options: Yes — we've discussed it
They'd figure it out eventually
They'd have no idea where to start
questions.solution_tip: Your Resolved Brief IS the answer to this question. Hand it to them and they know exactly what to do first.
questions.gap_tip: This is literally what your Resolved Brief solves. When you hand it to your person, they'll have step one, step two, and every contact they need.

questions.prompt: Are your important documents stored in one known location — or scattered?
options: One secure, known location
A few different places
Scattered or I'm not sure where everything is

data_fields.placeholder: e.g., home safe, filing cabinet in office, fireproof box in closet...

questions.solution_tip: Documented. Your family goes to one place and finds everything.
questions.gap_tip: Your brief will be the first organized document your family has. Consider picking one spot — a safe, a filing cabinet — and moving everything there. 30 minutes, done.

questions.prompt: Who is your estate attorney — and could your family reach them?
options: I have one and my family knows how to reach them
I have one but my family doesn't know who it is
I don't have one
questions.solution_tip: Your family can now reach your attorney with one phone call. That single contact unlocks access to your will, trust, and legal directives.
questions.gap_tip: No attorney yet? That's fine — not everyone needs one. For a basic will, online tools work. Your brief will note this so you can revisit it when ready.

sections.title: Key People & Decision Makers
sections.subtitle: Someone will have to step in. The question is whether they'll be ready — or drowning.
sections.intro_message: Now let's figure out who your people are — and whether they're actually prepared to step in.

questions.prompt: If something happened to you tonight, who would actually step in to handle everything?
options: One person — clearly defined and they know it
I have someone in mind, but we've never discussed it
No one — it would be chaos

data_fields.placeholder: e.g., wife Sarah, brother Mike, best friend Dave...

questions.solution_tip: Your point person is now documented with contact info. This goes on page one of your brief and on your Family Emergency Card.
questions.gap_tip: Pick one person — even if it's imperfect. Your Resolved Brief gives them everything they need to step in. You can always change it later.

questions.prompt: Would that person know where to start in the first 24 hours?
options: Yes — they have a clear starting point
They'd figure it out, but it would take time
They'd be completely lost
questions.solution_tip: Your Resolved Brief is their starting point. Page one tells them exactly what to do first.
questions.gap_tip: This is the #1 thing your Resolved Brief solves. By the time we're done, your person gets a step-by-step roadmap — not a pile of guesswork.

questions.prompt: If both you and your partner were gone at the same time, who steps in next?
options: Clearly defined backup
Someone would probably step up
No one — it would be a crisis on top of a crisis

data_fields.placeholder: e.g., sister Lisa, parents, close friend...

questions.solution_tip: Primary and backup are both documented. No ambiguity about who steps in.
questions.gap_tip: Name someone — even if you haven't talked to them yet. Your brief documents it, and the follow-up section will remind you to have the conversation.

questions.prompt: Have you actually told these people they're the ones?
options: Yes — we've had the conversation
I assumed they'd figure it out
No — I've never said it out loud
questions.solution_tip: Good — and now it's in writing too. No ambiguity.
questions.gap_tip: Your Resolved Brief makes this conversation easy. Hand it to them and say: 'I put this together for our family. You're listed as my point person.' Five minutes.

questions.prompt: Would your family know who to call for help — your attorney, CPA, financial advisor, insurance agent?
options: Yes — names and numbers are documented
They'd know some, but not all
They wouldn't know who any of these people are
questions.solution_tip: Your family's contact list is built. One page in your brief with every professional they'd need to call.
questions.gap_tip: Fill in whoever you have — skip what you don't. Even one name here saves your family hours of searching.

questions.prompt: Would the roles between your key people be clear — or would everyone step on each other?
options: Clear — everyone knows their role
Some overlap, but manageable
It would be confusing and likely cause conflict
questions.solution_tip: Your brief clarifies who does what. One point person, one backup, clear responsibilities.
questions.gap_tip: Conflict happens when no one knows who's in charge. Your brief assigns a clear point person — that alone prevents most of it.

questions.prompt: Does your family know your wishes well enough to speak for you confidently?
options: Yes — they could represent my wishes clearly
On some things, but not all
They'd be guessing
questions.solution_tip: And now your wishes are in writing — not just in someone's memory.
questions.gap_tip: By the end of this walkthrough, your wishes will be documented. Your family reads your brief instead of guessing.

questions.prompt: If a disagreement broke out between family members, is there a clear tiebreaker?
options: Yes — one person has final authority, documented
Informally understood
No — it would turn into a fight
questions.callout: Family conflict after a crisis is more common than people think. This one question prevents it.

data_fields.placeholder: e.g., wife Sarah has final authority on all decisions...

questions.solution_tip: Final authority is documented. If there's ever a dispute, your brief settles it.
questions.gap_tip: Name one person with final say. Even if it feels awkward, it prevents the kind of conflict that tears families apart.

questions.prompt: Could your key people access what they need — accounts, documents, contacts — within 48 hours?
options: Yes — everything is organized and accessible
Some things, but they'd have to dig
No — they'd be locked out of almost everything
questions.solution_tip: Your Resolved Brief IS that access point. Everything in one place.
questions.gap_tip: That's about to change. By the time we're done, your brief will point them to every account, document, and contact they need.

sections.title: Children & Dependents
sections.subtitle: If you weren't here tomorrow, would your children's world keep turning — or fall apart?
sections.intro_message: This section is for parents and caregivers. Skip it if it doesn't apply to you.

questions.prompt: Is there a clear, documented plan for who would care for your children?
options: Yes — legal guardianship is designated
I've thought about it but nothing is official
No plan exists

data_fields.placeholder: e.g., sister Lisa and her husband Mark...

questions.solution_tip: Your guardian is documented in your brief. Your family knows exactly who steps in.
questions.gap_tip: This is the single most important decision for parents. Name someone now — even informally — and your brief will document it. Making it legal takes one conversation with an attorney.

questions.prompt: Is there a backup guardian if your first choice can't do it?
options: Yes — identified and documented
I have someone in mind
No backup
questions.solution_tip: Primary and backup guardians documented. Your children are covered.
questions.gap_tip: Name a backup — even someone imperfect. Having two options means your children are never in limbo.

questions.prompt: Have you told the guardian they've been chosen — and do they understand what it means?
options: Yes — we've had a real conversation about it
I've mentioned it casually
No — they don't know
questions.solution_tip: Good — and it's now in writing too.
questions.gap_tip: Your brief makes this conversation easier. Hand it to them: 'You're listed as guardian for our kids if something happens. Here's everything you'd need.'

questions.prompt: Could someone continue your children's daily routines — school, activities, medical, meals?
options: Yes — it's all documented or well-known
Some of it, but there are gaps
They'd be starting from scratch
questions.solution_tip: Your children's key contacts are in your brief. Their world keeps turning.
questions.gap_tip: Fill in what you can — school and doctor are the two most critical. Your brief will keep their world running.

questions.prompt: Are your children's critical details easy to find — doctors, allergies, medications, school contacts?
options: Yes — organized and accessible
Scattered across different places
Only in my head
questions.solution_tip: All captured in your brief. A caregiver has everything they need on day one.
questions.gap_tip: Getting this out of your head and onto paper is the whole point. Your brief now has it.

questions.prompt: Would someone know how to emotionally support your children through this — therapist contacts, special needs, comfort routines?
options: Yes — documented or well-communicated
Partially
No — no one would know what they need
questions.solution_tip: Emotional support contacts documented. Your children get continuity, not just logistics.
questions.gap_tip: Even noting 'no current therapist' is helpful. If a caregiver needs to find one, knowing to start fresh saves time.

questions.prompt: Would your children's future — education, values, traditions — be guided by your wishes or guessed at?
options: Guided by my clearly stated wishes
Somewhat — people close to me would try
Mostly guessed
questions.callout: This is the one that keeps parents up at night. If you haven't written it down, you're leaving your children's future to interpretation.
questions.solution_tip: Your wishes are documented. Your values live on in writing.
questions.gap_tip: The last section of this walkthrough gives you space to write exactly this. Your words, your values, preserved.

questions.prompt: Are there financial provisions specifically for your children — education funds, trusts, designated accounts?
options: Yes — clearly set up and documented
Some, but incomplete
Nothing specific

data_fields.prompt: What accounts exist for your children?
data_fields.placeholder: e.g., 529 plan at Vanguard, UTMA at Schwab...

questions.solution_tip: Children's financial accounts documented. A guardian knows exactly what's available for their care and education.
questions.gap_tip: If this is a gap, your brief's follow-up section will include how to set up a 529 education plan (takes 15 minutes online).

sections.title: Money, Assets & Obligations
sections.subtitle: Bills don't stop. Mortgages don't pause. Could your family keep the lights on?
sections.intro_message: Now let's look at the money. Not the numbers — just whether your family could find it and manage it without you.

questions.prompt: If your family needed money within 7 days, would they know exactly where to get it?
options: Yes — clear access to accounts
They'd eventually figure it out
They'd have no idea where to start

data_fields.prompt: Primary bank account (the one most money flows through)?
Is anyone else on this account?

questions.solution_tip: Your primary bank is documented. If there's a joint holder, they can access funds immediately. If not, your brief flags what your family needs to do.
questions.gap_tip: Let's fix that right now. Just the bank name — no account numbers needed. Your family needs to know where to go, not the digits.

questions.prompt: How easy would it be for someone to find ALL of your accounts — banking, retirement, investments, crypto?
options: Everything documented in one place
Some are documented, others aren't
It would be a scavenger hunt

data_fields.prompt: List all accounts your family should know about:
data_fields.placeholder: e.g., Chase checking, Chase savings, Fidelity 401k, Vanguard IRA, Coinbase...

questions.solution_tip: Complete account inventory documented. No scavenger hunt.
questions.gap_tip: This is the single most valuable thing you can do right now. Every account you list here is one your family won't have to discover by accident.

questions.prompt: Are there accounts your family doesn't even know exist?
options: No — they know about everything
Possibly one or two
Yes — definitely accounts they'd never find
questions.callout: Billions in retirement funds, life insurance, and bank accounts go unclaimed every year. This is why.

data_fields.prompt: Any accounts only you know about?
data_fields.placeholder: e.g., old 401k from previous employer, savings account at a different bank, HSA...

questions.solution_tip: No hidden accounts — your family has the full picture.
questions.gap_tip: Now they're not hidden anymore. Every account you list here goes into your brief — found, not forgotten.

questions.prompt: Could someone keep your bills running — mortgage, utilities, insurance premiums, subscriptions?
options: Yes — everything is documented with autopay details
They'd figure out most of it
Bills would start failing within weeks

data_fields.placeholder: e.g., mortgage (Chase), electric (ComEd), car insurance (Geico), internet (Xfinity)...
e.g., water bill, property taxes, HOA...

questions.solution_tip: Bill map documented. Your family knows what's on autopay and what needs manual attention.
questions.gap_tip: Autopay keeps the lights on — but only if the card it's tied to stays active. Your brief will list everything so nothing falls through.

questions.prompt: Are your debts and obligations clearly outlined — mortgage, car loans, student loans, credit cards?
options: Yes — all documented
Some are known, others aren't
My family would discover debts they didn't know about

data_fields.prompt: List your debts (just the lender, not the amounts):
data_fields.placeholder: e.g., Wells Fargo mortgage, Toyota Financial car loan, Navient student loans, Chase Sapphire credit card...

questions.solution_tip: Debt inventory documented. Your family knows who to contact — and many loans have life insurance provisions they should ask about.
questions.gap_tip: Surprise debts are one of the worst things a family can discover. Listing them here means no surprises.

questions.prompt: Would someone know what you own — property, vehicles, investments, business interests?
options: Yes — a clear inventory exists
They'd know the big things but miss others
They'd have no idea what I own

data_fields.placeholder: e.g., house (123 Main St), 2022 Toyota Camry, rental property...

questions.solution_tip: Asset inventory documented. Nothing gets lost or forgotten.
questions.gap_tip: Every asset you name here is one your family won't accidentally lose or overlook.

questions.prompt: If you have business interests or side income, could someone manage or wind them down?
options: Yes — documented with instructions
Partially — some knowledge exists
No — it would collapse or be abandoned
N/A

data_fields.placeholder: e.g., JB Consulting LLC, partner Mike 555-0134...

questions.solution_tip: Business details documented. Your family or partner can step in or wind down properly.
questions.gap_tip: Even a one-line note about your business saves someone from having to piece it together.

questions.prompt: Would someone know who to contact for financial help — your banker, advisor, accountant?
options: Yes — names and contacts documented
They'd know one or two
They wouldn't know who to call
questions.solution_tip: Financial contacts already captured above. Your family has a phone list ready.
questions.gap_tip: Check if you listed these in the Key People section — if not, your brief's follow-up will remind you.

questions.prompt: Do beneficiary designations on your accounts match your current wishes?
options: Yes — recently reviewed and current
I'm not sure — haven't checked in a while
I don't know what's listed
questions.callout: Beneficiary designations override your will. An ex-spouse on a 401k gets the money — even if your will says otherwise.
questions.solution_tip: Good — your brief will note that beneficiaries are current.
questions.gap_tip: Your brief's follow-up section will flag this: log into each account and check the beneficiary. Most can be updated online in 5 minutes per account.

questions.prompt: Could your family handle your full financial picture — income, expenses, debts, assets — without major confusion?
options: Yes — it's organized and accessible
With effort and time
It would be overwhelming
questions.solution_tip: Your Resolved Brief IS the organized financial picture. Income, expenses, debts, assets — all in one document.
questions.gap_tip: It won't be overwhelming anymore. Everything you've entered in this section is now organized in your brief.

sections.title: Insurance & Protection
sections.subtitle: Insurance only works if someone files a claim. If they don't know it exists, it's worthless.
sections.intro_message: Let's look at your safety net. Not just what you have — but whether your family could actually use it.

questions.prompt: Do you have life insurance?
options: Yes — through work, private policy, or both
I think so, but I'm not sure of the details
No

data_fields.placeholder: e.g., MetLife through work, Northwestern Mutual private policy...

questions.solution_tip: Life insurance details captured — provider, coverage, and beneficiary. Your family can file a claim on day one.
questions.gap_tip: No life insurance means your family has no financial cushion. A basic term policy can be set up online in 15 minutes — we recommend Ethos (ethoslife.com) for a fast, no-exam application. We'll include the link in your brief.

questions.prompt: If a life insurance claim needed to be filed, would someone know how?
options: Yes — provider, policy number, and claims process documented
They'd know I have it but would have to figure out the rest
No one would know where to start

data_fields.placeholder: e.g., filing cabinet, email from MetLife, agent has a copy...

questions.solution_tip: Claims roadmap is in your brief. Provider, location, who to call — done.
questions.gap_tip: Your brief now has the provider info. For the policy document, check your email — search the provider name. Your agent can also pull it with one call.

questions.prompt: Do you know your actual coverage amount — and would it cover your family for at least 2 years?
options: Yes — I know the amount and it's adequate
I know the amount but it might not be enough
I have no idea what my coverage is
questions.solution_tip: Coverage amount documented. Your family knows what to expect.
questions.gap_tip: Your brief's follow-up will flag this: call your provider or check your employer benefits portal. Takes 5 minutes to confirm your coverage amount.

questions.prompt: Are your other insurance policies documented — health, home, auto, disability, umbrella?
options: Yes — all policies organized in one place
Some are, some aren't
My family would have to call around to find them
questions.solution_tip: All policies captured in one place. Your family calls one number per policy and they're covered.
questions.gap_tip: Fill in what you know — even just the provider name. Your insurance agent can pull the rest.

questions.prompt: Do you have disability insurance — and do you know what it covers?
options: Yes — through work or privately, and I understand the coverage
I think I have it through work, but I'm not sure
No / I don't know
questions.solution_tip: Disability coverage documented. If you're ever incapacitated, your family knows income replacement is coming.
questions.gap_tip: Check with your employer's HR — many people have disability through work and don't realize it. Your brief will flag this.

questions.prompt: If you were incapacitated for 6 months, would your income be replaced?
options: Yes — disability coverage would kick in
Partially — some coverage but gaps exist
No — my family would be on their own financially
questions.callout: Most people insure their car but not their income. Your ability to earn is your most valuable asset.
questions.solution_tip: Your brief documents your income protection plan.
questions.gap_tip: Your brief will flag income protection as a follow-up item with specific options to explore.

questions.prompt: Could someone locate your insurance agent or broker?
options: Yes — contact info is documented
My family might be able to find them
No one knows who they are
questions.solution_tip: Your insurance agent is your family's single best resource. One call to them unlocks access to all your policies.
questions.gap_tip: If you have insurance, you have an agent. Check your policy docs or last premium statement for their info.

questions.prompt: Are beneficiaries on your insurance policies current — not an ex-spouse, deceased parent, or outdated name?
options: Yes — recently verified
I'm not sure — haven't checked
Likely outdated
questions.solution_tip: Beneficiaries verified and current. No surprises.
questions.gap_tip: Your brief's follow-up will flag this: call your provider or log in online to verify. 5 minutes per policy.

sections.title: Digital Life & Access
sections.subtitle: Your entire life is behind a passcode. If no one can get in, everything is lost.
sections.intro_message: Your phone, email, and passwords control everything. Let's see if your family could get in.

questions.prompt: Could someone you trust unlock your phone today?
options: Yes — they know the passcode
Maybe — I use biometrics and haven't shared the backup PIN
No — only I can get in
questions.solution_tip: Phone access confirmed. Your Vault Page will also have the passcode written down as backup — sealed in an envelope, never digital.
questions.gap_tip: Your phone unlocks 2FA codes, banking apps, and everything else. The Vault Page in your Resolved Brief has a dedicated line for your phone passcode — write it by hand, seal it in the envelope.

questions.prompt: If your email is the key to everything — password resets, bank statements, account recovery — could anyone get into it?
options: Yes — access is documented
They might be able to figure it out
No — it's completely locked
questions.solution_tip: Primary email documented. The password goes on your Vault Page — handwritten, sealed, never stored digitally.
questions.gap_tip: Email is the skeleton key to your digital life. Your brief documents the address, and the Vault Page is where the password goes — by hand, in a sealed envelope.

questions.prompt: Where are your passwords stored?
options: Password manager — and someone knows the master password
Scattered — some saved in browser, some on paper, some in my head
Only in my head
questions.solution_tip: Password system documented. Your Vault Page will have the master password — your family gets in with one key.
questions.gap_tip: Your Vault Page solves this for now — write your key passwords by hand and seal them in an envelope. Going forward, a password manager like 1Password (1password.com) keeps everything secure and shareable. We'll include the link in your brief.

questions.prompt: Could your family get into your computer?
options: Yes — they know the password
Maybe — it's usually unlocked
No — it's locked and no one has the password
questions.solution_tip: Computer access confirmed. Vault Page has the backup.
questions.gap_tip: Tax documents, financial software, saved files — they all live on your computer. Add the password to your Vault Page.

questions.prompt: Would your family be able to access your photos, videos, and irreplaceable digital memories?
options: Yes — they know how to access my cloud storage
Some things, but not everything
No — decades of memories would be gone
questions.callout: Photos can't be recovered. If they're locked in iCloud or Google Photos behind a password no one has, they're gone forever.

data_fields.placeholder: e.g., iCloud, Google Photos, external hard drive, computer...

questions.solution_tip: Photo locations documented. Your family knows where every memory lives.
questions.gap_tip: List where they are — your brief documents it. If your photos aren't backed up to the cloud yet, Google One (one.google.com) gives you 100GB for $1.99/mo. With the Vault Page passwords, your family can access everything.

questions.prompt: Are important documents stored in the cloud — and could your family access them?
options: Yes — Google Drive, Dropbox, iCloud — and access is documented
Some are in the cloud, but access isn't clear
I don't store things in the cloud / no one could get in

data_fields.placeholder: e.g., Google Drive, iCloud, Dropbox, OneDrive...

questions.solution_tip: Cloud storage documented. With email access via the Vault Page, your family can reach tax returns, contracts, everything.
questions.gap_tip: Your brief documents what exists. The Vault Page handles access.

questions.prompt: Do you have cryptocurrency, NFTs, or digital assets that require a private key or recovery phrase?
options: Yes — and recovery info is securely stored where someone could find it
Yes — but the recovery phrase is only known to me
No digital assets like this

data_fields.prompt: Platform and where is the recovery phrase?
data_fields.placeholder: e.g., Coinbase, seed phrase written in safe...

questions.solution_tip: Crypto access documented. Recovery phrase location goes on the Vault Page — never stored digitally.
questions.gap_tip: Crypto without a recovery phrase is gone FOREVER. Write the seed words on your Vault Page and seal it. This is not optional.

questions.prompt: What financial apps are on your phone — and could someone access them?
options: Documented — banking, Venmo, PayPal, investment apps are all known
They'd find some but miss others
No one would know what apps I use for money

data_fields.placeholder: e.g., Chase, Venmo, Robinhood, PayPal, CashApp...

questions.solution_tip: App inventory documented. With phone access, your family can reach every one of them.
questions.gap_tip: List them — even the small ones. Venmo balances, CashApp, PayPal — these add up and get forgotten.

questions.prompt: If every digital account you have was locked tomorrow, how much of your life would be inaccessible?
options: Very little — most things are documented offline too
A significant amount
Almost everything — my entire life is digital
questions.solution_tip: Your Resolved Brief is the offline backup to your digital life. If everything locks, this document gets your family started.
questions.gap_tip: That's exactly what your Resolved Brief and Vault Page solve. The brief documents what exists. The Vault Page unlocks it. Analog backup for a digital life.

sections.title: Medical & Final Wishes
sections.subtitle: When you can't speak for yourself, someone else will. Have you told them what to say?
sections.intro_message: Last section. This is where logistics meets legacy. Take your time.

questions.prompt: If you couldn't speak for yourself, who is making medical decisions — and do they know it?
options: Clearly defined, legally documented, and they know
Informally understood but nothing is legal
No one has been chosen
questions.solution_tip: Healthcare proxy documented with backup. This goes on your Family Emergency Card — the one-page reference your family brings to the ER.
questions.gap_tip: Name someone now. Your brief documents it, and to make it legal, your state has a free healthcare proxy form — takes 10 minutes. We'll include the link.

questions.prompt: Would that person know what you actually want — life support, resuscitation, organ donation?
options: Yes — we've had the conversation and it's written down
We've talked about it loosely
They'd be guessing in a hospital hallway
Full resuscitation (do everything)
DNR (do not resuscitate)
Limited intervention
Want to discuss with doctor first
Yes
No
Undecided

questions.solution_tip: Your wishes are documented. Your family won't have to make this decision in a hospital hallway. That alone is worth this entire walkthrough.
questions.gap_tip: Answer these now — even 'undecided' is helpful. Your family carries the weight of these decisions if you don't make them.

questions.prompt: Is your medical history accessible — conditions, medications, allergies, doctors?
options: Yes — documented and easy to find
Partially — my spouse knows most of it
Only I know my full medical picture

data_fields.placeholder: e.g., high blood pressure, diabetes, asthma...

questions.solution_tip: Full medical profile captured. This goes directly onto your Family Emergency Card — the one your family hands to ER doctors.
questions.gap_tip: ER doctors need this immediately. Conditions, meds, allergies — fill in what you know. This goes on your Emergency Card.

questions.prompt: Would your family feel confident making medical decisions on your behalf — without guilt or doubt?
options: Yes — my wishes are clear enough to remove the burden
Somewhat — but they'd still second-guess
No — they'd carry that weight forever
questions.callout: The guilt of making a wrong medical decision for a loved one never goes away. Clarity is the kindest thing you can give them.
questions.solution_tip: Your wishes are clear. You've given your family the gift of certainty.
questions.gap_tip: Everything you've answered in this section removes that burden. Your family reads your brief and knows exactly what you want.

questions.prompt: Have you expressed how you want to be remembered — the values, traditions, and words that matter most to you?
options: Yes — written down
I've mentioned some things in conversation
No — I've never put it into words

data_fields.prompt: What kind of service would you want?
options: Traditional funeral
Celebration of life
Small private gathering
No service
Let my family decide
Burial
Cremation
Green/natural burial
Donate body to science
No preference

questions.solution_tip: Your preferences are documented. Your family gets to focus on honoring you instead of debating what you would have wanted.
questions.gap_tip: Even 'no preference' or 'let my family decide' is a gift — it gives them permission to choose without guilt.

questions.prompt: Would your funeral or memorial preferences be followed — or argued about?
options: Followed — clearly documented
Mostly followed — people have a general idea
Argued about — no one knows what I'd want

data_fields.placeholder: e.g., specific songs, readings, no flowers — donate instead, specific people to notify...

questions.solution_tip: Documented. No arguments, no guessing.
questions.gap_tip: Write what matters. Even one line — a song, a location, a request — gives your family something to hold onto.

questions.prompt: Is there anything you want your family to know — a message, a letter, something only you can say — that you haven't written down yet?
options: I've written it
I've thought about it but haven't done it
I haven't — but I should
questions.callout: This is the last question for a reason. Everything above is logistics. This is legacy. Don't leave the most important thing unsaid.

data_fields.placeholder: This is your space. Say what matters most to you. Your family will read this over and over.

questions.solution_tip: You said it. It's in writing. Your family will carry these words with them.
questions.gap_tip: Take your time. There's no wrong answer. Whatever you write here is the page your family will read over and over.

emotional_close.headline: Take a breath. You just did it.
emotional_close.body: You just did what most families put off for years — and never get around to. Everything you entered is now organized, structured, and ready to protect the people you love.

score_reveal.before_intro: When you started, your family's preparedness looked like this:
score_reveal.current_intro: After building your Resolved Brief together:
score_reveal.projected_intro: And once you knock out the few items below —
score_reveal.no_followups_message: You're fully resolved. Nothing left to do but print it, fill out your Account & Access sheet, and tell one person where the envelope is.

emotional_close.quote: There's an envelope in my desk.
emotional_close.quote_sub: You're about to be able to say that.

follow_up_section.title: What's Left — And How To Finish It
follow_up_section.subtitle: Your Resolved Brief handles the heavy lifting. These are the few things that need one more step — with exactly how to do each one.
follow_up_section.design_note: Dynamically generated based on assessment scores. Only show items where a gap was identified (score of 0 or 1). Each item includes the specific action AND the resource to complete it.

items.solution: FreeWill.com (free) or Trust & Will ($159) — takes about 20 minutes online. No lawyer needed for a basic will.
Your state has a free form — search '[your state] power of attorney form.' Fill it out, sign with a witness.
Search '[your state] healthcare proxy form' — free, takes 10 minutes. Your brief already has the person's name.
Log into each financial account and check the beneficiary. Most can be updated online in 5 minutes per account.

items.item: Add a joint holder or POD to your primary bank account
items.solution: Call your bank and ask to add a POD (payable on death) beneficiary. This lets your family access funds immediately without probate.

items.item: Set up Emergency Access in your password manager
items.solution: 1Password: Settings → Emergency Kit. LastPass: Settings → Emergency Access. Bitwarden: Settings → Emergency Access. Add your trusted person.

items.item: Back up photos to a second location
items.solution: Turn on iCloud Photos or Google Photos auto-backup. Free up to 15GB (Google) or 5GB (iCloud).
Print your Resolved Brief. On the Vault Page, write by hand: phone passcode, email password, computer password, and password manager master key. Seal in an envelope. Store with your brief.

items.item: Tell your point person they've been chosen
items.solution: Hand them your Resolved Brief and say: 'I put this together for our family. You're listed as my point person.' That's the whole conversation.

items.item: Tell someone where your Resolved Brief is kept
items.solution: Your brief is only useful if someone can find it. Tell one person: 'There's an envelope in [location]. If anything happens to me, start there.'
Set a calendar reminder. Open your brief, scan for anything that's changed. Most updates take 5 minutes.

follow_up_section.closing_message: You don't have to do it all today. You already did the hard part.

scoring.description: Three scores tell the transformation story: where you started, where the brief brought you, and where you'll be after completing the few remaining items.

before_scoring.label: Where You Started
before_scoring.description: Raw assessment score based on tap answers only — this is the wake-up call.

current_scoring.label: Where You Are Now
current_scoring.description: Boosted by every data field completed during the walkthrough. If user tapped 0 but filled in the data field, their score for that question becomes 2.
current_scoring.rule: If a question has data_fields AND at least one data_field is filled in, the current_score for that question = 2 regardless of what they tapped.

projected_scoring.label: After Your Follow-Ups
projected_scoring.description: Always 100%. The follow-up items are the only remaining gap. Each has a specific action, link, and time estimate. Completing them closes every gap.
projected_scoring.rule: projected_score = max_possible. The follow-up section contains ONLY the items needed to reach 100%. Show the count and total time.
projected_scoring.message_template: Complete these {count} items ({total_time}) and you're fully resolved.

display.message_template: You came in at {before_pct}%. Your Resolved Brief brought you to {current_pct}%. Complete the {followup_count} items below and you're at 100%.

vault_page.title: Account & Access Sheet — Handwritten Only
vault_page.subtitle: Print this page. Fill it out BY HAND. Seal in an envelope. Store with your Resolved Brief.
vault_page.instructions: This page contains the sensitive details your Resolved Brief deliberately does NOT store digitally. Your brief tells your family WHERE to go — this page tells them HOW to get in.
vault_page.warning: NEVER photograph, scan, or store this page digitally. Handwritten + sealed envelope = secure. Replace this page if any info changes.
vault_page.footer: This page + your Resolved Brief = everything your family needs. Tell ONE person where the envelope is.
